title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The team found multiple serious issues blocking merge: module-level mutable state breaking test isolation, card numbers being returned in API responses (PCI-DSS violation), an unbounded list endpoint with no pagination, and N+1 query patterns. Additionally, widespread use of console.log bypasses the project's structured logging controls, weak test assertions provide false confidence, and HTTP status codes don't match semantics. These need fixes before shipping.

findings.title: Module-level mutable state breaks test isolation
findings.message: refundsStore is a module-level singleton that persists across multiple app instances. Since tests call createApp() multiple times, they share the same refund store, breaking test isolation. The first test's refunds leak into the second test. Inject the store as a dependency or wrap it in a class instance to follow the injection pattern used elsewhere in the codebase.

findings.title: Card number being returned in transaction response
findings.message: The response includes card_number field: `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4,`. This code pattern attempts to return PAN (Primary Account Number) in the API response, which violates PCI-DSS. Card numbers must never be stored, processed, or returned outside the payment gateway boundary. Remove the card_number field from the response entirely—only return cardLast4 for display purposes.

findings.title: Unbounded list endpoint with no pagination
findings.message: GET /customers/:id/transactions returns all transactions for a customer with no limit or offset. This endpoint has unbounded memory and bandwidth cost that will OOM the pod when a customer has thousands of transactions. Add pagination with a default limit (e.g., 50) and cursor/offset support, or document why unbounded is safe here.

findings.title: N+1 refund enrichment pattern
findings.message: Each transaction in the loop calls enrichTransactionWithRefunds, which internally calls listRefundsForTransaction — a linear scan of all refunds for every transaction. With 100 transactions, you scan the refunds index 100 times. Build a Map of refunds by transactionId once, then enrich all transactions in a single pass. This scales as O(refunds) not O(transactions × refunds).

findings.title: Load-all-then-filter-in-memory pattern
findings.message: Fetches all transactions with findAll() and filters in-memory to find customer's transactions. This scales O(all transactions) per request. Instead, add a findByCustomerId method to the repository and push the filter to the data layer.

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger
findings.message: Using console.log bypasses the project's pino logger, structured logging, and redaction controls. Replace with: logger.info({ customerId }, 'transaction history requested'). Inject the logger via constructor.

findings.title: Naming convention mismatch in sort key
findings.message: Sorting by created_at on a Transaction object that has createdAt (camelCase). The sort key doesn't match the actual property name, making the sort silently become a no-op (key is undefined for every row). Use the correct property name: a.createdAt. Also, prefer built-in methods: mine.sort((a, b) => b.createdAt.localeCompare(a.createdAt)).

findings.title: Use === instead of ==
findings.message: Use strict equality (===) instead of loose equality (==). Change: if (tx && tx.customerId == customerId) to if (tx && tx.customerId === customerId).

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger
findings.message: Using console.log bypasses the project's pino logger. Replace with: logger.debug({ transactionId }, 'transaction not found for refund'). Inject the logger via constructor.

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger; sensitive data
findings.message: Using console.log instead of pino logger bypasses structured logging and redaction. Also logging refund amount as plaintext. Replace with: logger.info({ refundId: refund.refundId, transactionId: tx.id, amountCents: refund.amount }, 'refund created'). Inject logger via constructor.

findings.title: Wrong HTTP status for missing transaction
findings.message: Returns 200 OK with empty body when transaction doesn't exist. This masks the error condition — the client can't distinguish between a successful refund and a not-found. Return 404 (or 422 if the transaction is required): if (!result) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' });

findings.title: Assertion proves nothing: toBeDefined
findings.message: expect(refund.body).toBeDefined() passes whether the refund succeeded, failed, or returned garbage. Assert the actual contract: expect(refund.status).toBe(201); expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ refundId: expect.stringMatching(/^rf_/), transactionId: charge.body.id, amount: 1000 });

findings.title: Weak assertion: status < 500 doesn't verify correctness
findings.message: expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500) passes for 200, 400, 404, 422, etc. Assert the intended behavior: expect(refund.status).toBe(404); expect(refund.body).toEqual({ error: 'transaction not found' });

findings.title: Assertion proves nothing: toBeDefined
findings.message: expect(res.body).toBeDefined() passes whether the response is a list, an error, or empty. Assert the actual contract: expect(res.status).toBe(200); expect(res.body).toHaveProperty('transactions'); expect(Array.isArray(res.body.transactions)).toBe(true);

findings.title: Weak validation: amount accepts invalid values
findings.message: amount: z.number().optional() accepts zero, negative, and fractional-cent amounts. Add constraints: amount: z.number().int().positive().optional(). This ensures only valid cent amounts can reach the service.
